coredata.dc:title: Osmanthus-derived carbon dots for cell imaging and NIR photothermal therapy

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs)-based photothermal transduction agents are considered biosafety nanomaterials for photothermal therapy (PTT) owing to their excellent biocompatibility, outstanding photostability, and superior solubility. Despite many efforts, CDs with photothermal capacities were also limited. Herein, fluorescence CDs with outstanding near-infrared (NIR) photothermal capacity were reported via a green method using natural osmanthus fragrans fruits as raw materials for cell imaging and PTT. The bright green fluorescent CDs can effectively enter into HeLa cells for bioimaging. Also, the CDs exhibited negligible toxicity, considerable NIR absorption, high photothermal conversion efficiency (808 nm, 46.7 %), and excellent photothermal stability. In vitro PTT experimental results showed that CDs can induce HeLa cell death upon 808 nm laser irradiation due to their outstanding photothermal performances. Thus, this work provides a good paradigm for the development of biocompatible CDs-based photothermal agents for PTT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic depiction of the synthetic procedure of CDs. (b) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (e) C 1 s, (f) N 1 s and (g) O 1 s XPS spectra of CDs.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs. (b) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of CDs. (c) CLSM images of HeLa cells treated with CDs.

(a) UV–Vis–NIR spectra of CDs. (b) Photothermal heating curves and (c) infrared thermal images of CDs solutions under 808 nm laser irradiation. (d) Heating and cooling curves of CDs and the corresponding plots of cooling time. (e) Photothermal stability of the CDs.

(a) Cell viability of HeLa and L929 cells. (b) Cell viability of HeLa cells with different treatments. (c) Schematic illustration of PTT in vitro. (d) Calcein-AM/PI co-stained HeLa cells with different treatments. (e) Apoptosis and necrosis analysis toward HeLa cells after various treatments.

head.ce:title: Osmanthus-derived carbon dots for cell imaging and NIR photothermal therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, International Joint Research Laboratory for Biomedical Nanomaterials of Henan, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photothermal carbon dots with outstanding biocompatibility were prepared via a green method using natural osmanthus fragrans fruits as raw materials for cell imaging and NIR PTT.

Carbon dots (CDs)-based photothermal transduction agents are considered biosafety nanomaterials for photothermal therapy (PTT) owing to their excellent biocompatibility, outstanding photostability, and superior solubility. Despite many efforts, CDs with photothermal capacities were also limited. Herein, fluorescence CDs with outstanding near-infrared (NIR) photothermal capacity were reported via a green method using natural osmanthus fragrans fruits as raw materials for cell imaging and PTT. The bright green fluorescent CDs can effectively enter into HeLa cells for bioimaging. Also, the CDs exhibited negligible toxicity, considerable NIR absorption, high photothermal conversion efficiency (808 nm, 46.7 %), and excellent photothermal stability. In vitro PTT experimental results showed that CDs can induce HeLa cell death upon 808 nm laser irradiation due to their outstanding photothermal performances. Thus, this work provides a good paradigm for the development of biocompatible CDs-based photothermal agents for PTT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photothermal therapy (PTT) has garnered significant attention in the field of cancer treatment due to its non-invasiveness and precise targeting [1,2]. Recently, extensive investigations have been conducted to develop diverse photothermal transduction agents (PTAs) for PTT including inorganic nanoparticles and organic materials [3,4]. However, most PTAs often face challenges due to their poor biocompatibility and biotoxicity.

Carbon dots (CDs), a newly discovered carbon nanomaterial with zero dimensions, have attracted considerable attention due to their tunable electrical structure, high photostability, and negligible cytotoxicity [5,6]. These properties give them significant potential for applications in bioimaging, sensing, diagnosis, and treatment of cancer [7–9]. In particular, certain CDs are capable of converting light energy into heat via a nonradiative relaxation process [10,11]. Green synthesis of CDs derived from biomass has garnered much attention due to their non-toxicity and inherent advantages over chemical synthesis methods [12]. Despite much effort has been devoted to developing various CDs for PTT, the reported biomass-derived CDs were also limited [1,13–15]. Consequently, it is desirable to explore a cost-effective and green strategy for developing CDs with considerable photothermal conversion capacities.

Herein, Osmanthus-derived CDs with outstanding photothermal capacity and bright fluorescence were reported via a green and low-cost method. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the synthesis of photothermal CDs from natural Osmanthus fragrant fruits. The green fluorescent CDs were able to efficiently enter the HeLa cells for cellular imaging. Moreover, the CDs exhibited desirable absorption capacity in the near-infrared region (NIR, 700–850 nm) and could convert NIR light into heat. In vitro PTT experimental results demonstrated that the CDs could effectively induce the apoptosis of tumor cells upon 808 nm laser irradiation.

Typically, the Osmanthus fragrant fruits were washed several times with water, and the pulp was collected. Then, 100 μL of ethylenediamine (EAN) was added to the osmanthus fragrans juice (5 mL), and the mixed solution was transferred to a steel autoclave containing 25 mL of deionized water. After heating for 12 h at 180 °C, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antitumor performance of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and cultured for 12 h. Then, 50 μL of CDs (1 mg/mL) was added to each well and cultured for 4 h. After irradiation with an 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) for 10 min, the cells were incubated for 24 h, and the cell viability was determined by MTT assay. Also, the HeLa cells were co-stained with Calcein-AM/PI to evaluate the cell activity. For flow cytometric analysis, the cells were stained with Annexin V-FITC and PI.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CDs were obtained via a facile one-pot hydrothermal route using economically and easily available osmanthus as raw materials using EAN as N dopant. The TEM image showed that CDs possessed a uniform diameter of about 3.6 nm (Fig. 1b). Moreover, a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was observed (inset of Fig. 1b), which was assigned to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon materials [16]. The XRD pattern of CDs showed a broad peak around 2θ ≈ 20.9° (Fig. 1c), corresponding to the (002) plane of CDs. Fig. 1d shows the FT-IR spectra of CDs. As shown, the bands at 1659 and 1080 cm−1 were assigned to C=O and C–O–C, respectively. The absorption bands at 3430 and 1410 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H and C–N [16,17], respectively. The XPS spectrum of CDs revealed that the CDs mainly consisted of C (70.4 a.t.%), N (14.1 a.t.%), and O (15.5 a.t.%) (Fig. S1). The XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 1e) presented five peaks at 287.85, 286.85, 285.05, 284.75, and 283.7 eV, corresponding to the C=O, O–C=O, C–N, C=C/C–C, and C–O bonds, respectively. For N1s, it exhibited four peaks at 398.7, 399.1, 399.8, and 400.8 eV (Fig. 1f), corresponding to the pyrrolic N (21.6 at.%), aminic N (22.9 at.%), pyridinic N (36.8 at.%), and graphitic N (17.49 at.%), respectively. The O 1 s XPS spectrum showed two peaks at 529.80, and 530.99 eV (Fig. 1g), which were assigned to the C–O/C–O–C and C=O bands, respectively.

The fluorescence spectra of the CDs showed a green fluorescent emission centered at 500 nm with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm (Fig. 2 a). Also, an excitation-dependent fluorescence was observed (Fig. 2b), possibly due to the existence of emissive trap sites on the CDs.[18] Moreover, the fluorescence intensity almost remained steady after continuous irradiation with a UV lamp for 180 min (Fig. S2), indicating the good anti-photobleaching performance of CDs. Moreover, the CDs also showed good fluorescence stability in salinity solutions (Fig. S3). Fig. 2c shows the CLSM images of HeLa cells after incubation with CDs. As shown, bright green fluorescence appeared in HeLa cells, demonstrating that CDs can effectively enter the cells for bioimaging.

We then investigated the photothermal conversion performance of CDs. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–Vis-NIR spectra of CDs with different concentrations. As shown, the CDs showed a broad absorption (700–850 nm) in the NIR region. Fig. 3b-c showed the photothermal conversion performances of CDs under 808 nm laser irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3b, upon 808 nm laser irradiation, the temperatures of CDs solution exhibited a concentration-dependent increase. The temperature increased rapidly by approximately 28 °C after irradiation for 15 min when the CDs solution reached 2.0 mg/mL. Additionally, a higher laser power density led to higher temperature elevation (Fig. 3c). The calculated photothermal conversion efficiency was 46.7 % (Fig. 3d), possibly due to the presence of plenty of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N [11,14]. Furthermore, the CDs exhibited good photothermal stability after four heat-cool cycles (Fig. 3e). These findings demonstrated that the CDs can effectively convert NIR light to heat under 808 nm light irradiation.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of CDs, normal mouse L929 fibroblast cells and HeLa cells were incubated with CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, no significant toxicity was observed when the concentration of CDs reached 1.2 mg/mL, indicating the outstanding biocompatibility of CDs. In contrast, the cell viability showed a significant decrease after the HeLa cells were further irradiated with an 808 nm laser (Fig. 4b, c). To distinguish the live and dead cells, the cells were co-stained with Calcein-AM and PI (Fig. 4d). As shown, intense red fluorescence appeared in the CDs + NIR group, whereas green fluorescence appeared in the other groups, indicating that HeLa cells can be effectively killed by CDs due to the heat effect of CDs under 808 nm laser irradiation. Furthermore, the cell death pathways induced by CDs under NIR irradiation were investigated by flow cytometry analysis (Fig. 4e). As shown, the CDs + NIR group showed noticeable cell apoptosis ratio (67.5 %) due to the heat effect of CDs, whereas the other control groups did not exhibit obvious cell death. These results confirm that CDs can induce the apoptosis of tumor cells under NIR irradiation.

In summary, we for the first time developed osmanthus-derived CDs with outstanding photothermal conversion capacities and excellent biocompatibility for cell imaging and NIR PTT. The CDs could be used as fluorescence probes for cellular imaging. Moreover, the obtained CDs showed strong absorption in the NIR region and good photothermal conversion performance upon 808 nm laser irradiation. Further, the CDs showed effective photothermal toxicity on cancer cells due to their photothermal conversion capacity, thereby causing apoptosis of tumor cells.

Shaohua Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Han Cui: Investigation, Data curation. Jingbin Huang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Boshi Tian: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration. Jianchun Bao: Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Key Program of Committee of Education of Henan Province (21A150065) and the Key Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province (242102241034).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.137347.
